questions.text: I believe that life rewards those who show up consistently, day after day.
I hold the view that decisive action is a form of respect for everyone's time.
I hold the view that composure under emotional pressure is the characteristic that most consistently distinguishes clear-headed decision-makers from reactive ones.
I find it natural to give instructions and coordinate others.
I feel that I have a long fuse — it takes a lot to provoke a strong emotional response in me.
I find that I am more interested in the factual accuracy of what is said than in how it was received.
I sense when someone is being polite out of obligation rather than genuine warmth.
I hold the view that treating suffering as morally significant — even when it is one concern among many — is what keeps my priorities aligned with my values.
I tend to propose ideas and invite feedback before moving forward.
I let each morning develop its own rhythm instead of front-loading priority tasks.
I believe that recognizing the urgency of another's suffering — not just its existence — is what distinguishes deep moral engagement from surface-level concern.
I experience the pressure of a deadline not just mentally but as a tightening sensation throughout my body.
I find that I can participate in emotionally intense situations without my own system getting activated.
I experience the feeling of accomplishment as a quiet satisfaction rather than a triumphant high.
I practice important skills repeatedly until they become second nature.
I find that setting aggressive personal deadlines keeps me sharp and engaged.
I prefer to let others take credit even when I did most of the work.
I notice when a conversation partner's pupils dilate or contract in response to what is being discussed.
I believe that when I succeed, the first thing I should do is figure out how to bring others along.
I find that my system responds to emotional stimuli the way a tuning fork responds to sound — instantly and completely.
I hold the view that collecting meaningful personal possessions adds richness to my life.
I treat every commitment I make as a binding agreement with myself.
I give others a quick overview and trust them to figure out the details their own way.
I feel that the moral significance of suffering should not be treated as subject to negotiation or compromise.
When I get good news, the euphoria can last for days.
I want to be the one who launches new ventures within my team.
I find that approaching others' difficulties with analytical detachment allows me to construct plans that address the problem systematically rather than piecemeal.
I feel that my willingness to accommodate helps groups stay together.
I tend to focus on the clarity of a message rather than on what the sender might be feeling.
I hold the view that groups find focus naturally when everyone's voice is heard.
I tend to wait until the pressure of a deadline sharpens my focus.
I routinely make lifestyle choices that reflect my personal standards and aspirations.
I believe my physical energy should be spent on tasks that make life easier for those around me.
I notice the subtle ways people claim or yield conversational space.
I think that those who can remain analytical during emotionally intense situations provide a kind of stability that is essential for groups.
I find that I do not track who interrupted whom during a meeting.
